coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal growth of nitrogen-rich carbon dots as a precise multifunctional probe for both Fe3+ detection and cellular bio-imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Ying
Liu, Yamin
Li, Siyuan
Lu, Bin
Liu, Cailin
Yang, Haijun
Ren, Xianyan
Hou, Yiling
coredata.dc:description: A precise probe that can be applied in a variety of situations, such as both environmental sensor and in vitro imaging, is very attractive and promising. Here, we firstly reported a kind of nitrogen-rich carbon dots (N-CDots) growing during hydrothermal treatment, which emit bright and colorful fluorescence can be utilized as a reliable probe for Ferric ions (Fe3+) detection and bio-imaging. The nitrogen/carbon molar ratio (N/C) in N-CDots is as high as 39.9%. It is the high nitrogen content which provides N-CDots high quantum yield (at excitation wavelength of 365 nm, 400 nm, 440 nm: 34.8%, 27.6%, 22.3%) and thus high sensitivity and bright imaging when detecting Fe3+ and labeling cells, respectively. Regards to Fe3+ detection, the N-CDots show really low detection limit (7.6 nM), high selectivity when 16 kinds of other cationic ions are present and high accuracy to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in tap water. Regards to bio-imaging, the N-CDots showing low toxicity, are successfully loaded into cells and emit colorful florescence in cytoplasm and cytoderm. Furthermore, the N-CDots possess good water dispersibility, excellent photochemical stability and excitation dependent behavior, revealing the big potential for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multifunctional probe
Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of N-CDots for detecting Fe3+ tap water
In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of N-CDots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This work was supported by the Southwest University of Science and Technology of the Startup Foundation for Doctors, China [ 16ZX7139 ]; the longshan Talent Program, China [ 17LZX675 ]; and Postgraduate Innovation Fund Project by Southwest University of Science and Technology, China [ 18YCX069 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) is transmission electron microscopy (TME) images of N-CDots assembled on Cu grid. Inset: X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of the N-CDots, (b) FTIR spectrum of N-CDots.
Cartoon illustration on N-CDots in this work which are GLU-resulted carbon core with MH-dominant surface as a passivation layer.
The UV–vis spectrum and excitation-dependent (from 400 nm to 560 nm) fluorescence spectra of N-CDots, inset: the normalized emission spectra.

The stability of fluorescence intensity of N-CDots and Rhodamine B at 365 nm excitation (a) in different pH solutions, (b) in different concentrations of NaCl from 0.1 M to 1.0 M, (c) at different temperature ranging from 5 °C to 55 °C, (d) under continuous UV irradiation for 120 min (record for every 10 min).

(a) Effect of different metal ions on the fluorescent intensity of N-CDots in aqueous solution, (b) relative fluorescence response of Fe3+ (F0/F) versus concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 50 μM. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDots in the absence and presence of Fe3+.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of N-CDots with an excitation at 440 nm in the absence (black) and in the presence of Fe3+ in the concentrations of 5 μM (red) and 50 μM (green). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence quenching mechanism schematic of N-CDots.

(a) In vitro cytotoxicity testing results of N-CDots with various concentrations against 3T3 and HepG2 cells from a CCk-8 assay, (b) fluorescent images of N-CDots through three different color filters under UV Light at 365 nm, (c) fluorescent microscopic image of CT26.WT cells incubated in PBS buffer with 200 mg mL−1 of N-CDots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of the performance of the as-prepared N-CDots with some representative reported carbon dots in the detection of Fe3+.
Fluorescence lifetimes obtained with double-exponential fit of the fluorescence decay curves of the N-CDots, N-CDots/Fe3+(5 μM), and N-CDots/Fe3+(50 μM), respectively.
Determination of Fe3+ in tap water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of the three times measured samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Standard addition method results for the determination of Fe3+ in tap water samples.
Comparisons of the fluorescence lifetime of the as-prepared N-CDots with some representative carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal growth of nitrogen-rich carbon dots as a precise multifunctional probe for both Fe3+ detection and cellular bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, China West Normal University, Nanchong, 637002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Clemson University, South Carolina, 29634, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A precise probe that can be applied in a variety of situations, such as both environmental sensor and in vitro imaging, is very attractive and promising. Here, we firstly reported a kind of nitrogen-rich carbon dots (N-CDots) growing during hydrothermal treatment, which emit bright and colorful fluorescence can be utilized as a reliable probe for Ferric ions (Fe3+) detection and bio-imaging. The nitrogen/carbon molar ratio (N/C) in N-CDots is as high as 39.9%. It is the high nitrogen content which provides N-CDots high quantum yield (at excitation wavelength of 365 nm, 400 nm, 440 nm: 34.8%, 27.6%, 22.3%) and thus high sensitivity and bright imaging when detecting Fe3+ and labeling cells, respectively. Regards to Fe3+ detection, the N-CDots show really low detection limit (7.6 nM), high selectivity when 16 kinds of other cationic ions are present and high accuracy to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in tap water. Regards to bio-imaging, the N-CDots showing low toxicity, are successfully loaded into cells and emit colorful florescence in cytoplasm and cytoderm. Furthermore, the N-CDots possess good water dispersibility, excellent photochemical stability and excitation dependent behavior, revealing the big potential for practical applications.

We reported a kind of nitrogen-rich carbon dots (N-CDots) growing during hydrothermal treatment emitting bright and colorful fluorescence, which can be utilized as a reliable probe for Fe3+ detection and biological imaging. The fluorescence quenching mechanism schematic of N-CDots is dynamic quenching and shows really low detection limit for Fe3+ at 7.6 nM.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have been extensively applied in various promising fields including bio-imaging, chemo-sensing, luminescent probes, diagnostic and therapeutic procedures [1,2]. Carbon dots (CDots), a typical kind of fluorescent nanomaterial have been researched in-depth due to their unique photochemical properties. As luminescent probes, CDots have shown their excellent performances in either metal ions [3], small molecules and noxious organics detection [4], or cell labeling and imaging [5]. Thus, it is expected that CDots have great potential as multifunctional probes and work very well in a verity of fields.

In this paper, we explored the capability of CDots as a multifunctional probe for both metal ions detection and cell imaging. On the one hand, Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the essential trace elements in environmental and biological systems and plays a crucial role in chemical and physiological processes in organisms [6], such as electron transport, nucleic acid synthesis, enzymatic catalysis, and cellular metabolism [3,5,7,8]. Determination of Fe3+ is undoubtedly important in a water environment and health monitoring. Thus, it is vital to develop a sensitive, selective and safe method to detect Fe3+ ion in food, clinical, human body and different industrial products.

Many techniques for the detection of Fe3+ have been proposed, such as electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [9], electrochemical [10], mass spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [11], spectrophotometric [12], and fluorescence spectroscopy [13]. Among the various detection methods, the fluorescence spectroscopic analysis based on fluorescent probe has drawn increasing attention because of its high sensitivity, simple operation, and excellent reproducibility.

On the other hand, cell or molecular-level imaging is an advanced biological technique for medical diagnosis [14,15], especially, the fluorescent molecular imaging, which has become one of the rapidly developing imaging technologies in recent years due to its high sensitivity, good repeatability and abundant information [16–19]. The fluorescent molecular imaging has been developing in many fields, such as life sciences, medicine and drug research to early diagnosis, biological therapies, early screening of drug candidates and study of malignant tumors [20,21].

CDots with high quality are crucial to our purpose. Here the hydrothermal method was employed to synthesis nitrogen-rich carbon dots (termed as N-CDots) due to its characters of low growth temperature [22]. The temperature is critical not only to the growth of carbon core but also to the nitrogen concentration in the passivation surface. The apparently successful in the hydrothermal method, as reflected by high nitrogen concentration and high quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDots, leading to N-CDots showing high sensitivity and bright imaging when detecting Fe3+ and labeling cells, respectively. Also highlighted and discussed are the applicability of N-CDots for testing the Fe3+ concentration in tap water and for in vitro imaging.

Metformin hydrochloride (MH) was obtained from Youcare Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Glucose anhydrous (GLU), Quinine sulfate and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Other reagents and metal salts (ZrCl4, KCl, ZnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, SrCl2·6H2O, SnCl2, Fe(Cl)3·6H2O, CaCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, Fe(Cl)2, LiCl, NiCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, NaCl and Pb(NO3)2) used in the experiments were acquired from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade without further purification and all the solutions were prepared with distilled deionized (DDI) water (obtained from Smart-Q15 Laboratory water purification system).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on TENSOR 27 (Bruker, America). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 with accelerating voltage of 200 kV) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'Pert PRO, PANalytical B) were employed to monitor the growth of carbon core. The content of nitrogen in N-CDots were measured by an elemental analyzer (Elementar, Germany). Fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (FL, Perkin Elmer, America) and an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Hitachi U2001, Japan), respectively.

Quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDots was determined by the comparative method using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY is 0.55 [23]) as a reference standard [24]. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDots with an excitation at 440 nm in the absence and in the presence of Fe3+ in the concentrations of 5 μM and 50 μM were measured on a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a time correlated single photon counting method. The data were deconvoluted with the instrument response function, recorded using dispersed light, and fitted to a double-exponential function.

The N-CDots were prepared according to our previous report [22]. Typically, 0.500 g of MH (nitrogen source) and 0.050 g of GLU (carbon source) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water then heated to 280 °C in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor over 2 h and kept at this temperature for another 2 h. Pure N-CDots were extracted from the obtained solution after filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed by MWCO of 5 kDa against DDI water for 24 h.

The detection of Fe3+ based on the fluorescent intensity of N-CDots was carried out on a spectrophotometer at room temperature in aqueous solution. The detection selectivity for Fe3+ ion was assessed against other 16 cations (Zr4+, K+, Zn2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Li+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, and Pb2+) which were dissolved in DDI water with the final concentration of 50 μM. The detection sensitivity for Fe3+ ion was confirmed by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of N-CDots solution (50 mg L−1) containing different concentration of Fe3+ (∼50 μM). After each cation solution was added to the N-CDots solution and mix thoroughly for 10 min, separately, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at 365 nm excitation (F). The fluorescence intensity of pure N-CDots solution (blank, F0) was recorded as well.

The accuracy of N-CDots for Fe3+detection was evaluated by employing tap water as the sample that contained known concentration of Fe3+ confirmed by an atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS, PerkinElmer). The tap water was standing for 72 h at 25 °C and then was filtered through a membrane filter (pore size 0.22 μm) to remove the impurities and gas. To avoid the precipitation of ferric ions and hydroxide ions in the test samples, the pH of the tap water was adjusted to 7.0 with the solution of HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). All detection experiments were performed at room temperature. The exciting slit and the emission slit were 3.0 nm both.

The cytotoxicity of the N-CDots on 3T3 and HepG2 cells was performed through cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [25]. Cell cultures were kept at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubated in dulbecco's modified medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics penicillin. The cells (1*104) were seeded on each well of 96 multiwell plates, and then different concentrations of N-CDots in DMEM (50、100、150、200、250、300、400、500、1000 μg mL−1) were added to each well at standard culture conditions for extra 24 h. After that, all the wells were carefully washed by phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.0) for five times. 20 mL of CCK-8 solution was then added to each well and incubated for another 4 h at 37 °C, the optical density (OD) of each well was measured with a Microplate Reader (Flex Station 3, Moleculardevices) at 450 nm. The cell viability (%)=(ODsample - ODblank)/(ODcontrol - ODblank) × 100% [26], where the subscript blank is the wells without cells.

CT26.WT cells were grown in roswell park memorial institute (RPMI) 1640 with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Cells (0.3*105) were seeded on cover slips in a tissue culture dish in RPMI 1640 culture medium (5 mL). When the confluent of culture dish was over 80%, N-CDots was added (the final concentration of N-CDots in RPMI is 200 μg mL−1) and incubation for 20 h. Then the medium was removed, the cells were washed with PBS solution for five times before being taken an image. The fluorescent images for CT26.WT cells were recorded on inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica DM13000, Germany) at the excitation of 480 nm, 560 nm and 720 nm.

Carbon dots are generally defined as small carbon nanoparticles (carbon core) with various surface passivation schemes (passivation shell) [27]. Mechanistically, the fluorescence from carbon dots is attributed to the presence of surface energy traps that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of the surface passivation [27]. Therefore, a desirable structure of the passivation molecular and the amount of it are very important, both determine the effectiveness the passivation shell surrounding the “carbon core” derived from carbonized GLU. As what have been discussed and reported previously [22], a deliberate designed mixture composed of GLU and enough amount of MH were dissolved in deionized water then treated by a hydrothermal method under a more robust condition than most of the “bottom-up” method [28] leading to N-CDots.

The N-CDots show observed absorption and bright fluorescence emission in visible light and other unique optical properties belonging to carbon dots, for example, excitation-dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 3 ) [34]. The quantum yield of N-CDots is 34.8% (at 365 nm excitation), 27.6% (at 400 nm excitation), and 22.3% (at 440 nm excitation), respectively.

The obtained N-CDots exhibited not only high quantum yields but also high stability. For example, the emission intensity of N-CDots excited at 365 nm remained almost constant after the N-CDots were exposed to solution with different pH and NaCl solution, under a range of temperatures and even irritated with 365 nm UV light, as shown in Fig. 4 . Especially, the ability of the N-CDots to resist photobleaching is stronger than Rhodamine B. The super stability of fluorescence guarantees the N-CDots’ great potential in sensing applications under various environmental conditions. Furthermore, the N-CDots are well water-soluble due to a great number of functional groups on its surface, such as NH2 and OH, which is propitious to the hydrophilicity of N-CDots [22].

In recent years, several fluorescence-based sensors have been developed for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions based on their surface-oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, which form complexes with metal ions. When applied to detect Fe3+, the N-CDots perform comparatively and even better. In terms of the selectivity, the N-CDots show pronounced fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+ but show little quenching in other representative cations when examined at 365 nm excitation, including Zr4+, K+, Zn2+, Co2+, Sr2+, Sn2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Al3+,Fe2+, Li+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, and Pb2+ (Fig. 5 a). This distinct effect may be attributed to the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ and N-CDots via nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on their surfaces [35].

In terms of the sensitivity, the PL intensity of N-CDots has linear negative correlation with the Fe3+ concentration sitting in 0 μM–50 μM, and low detection limit of 7.6 nM. The relative fluorescence response of N-CDots (F0/F ratio) is plotted a function of Fe3+ concentration from the Stern-Volmer equation in Fig. 4b. The calibration curve shows a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9942) between the F0/F ratio and Fe3+ concentration over Fe3+ concentrations of 0 μM–50 μM. The linear equation is as follows:
(1) F0/F = 0.04371X +0.9818
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDots in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, and X represents the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection was calculated based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal after being tested five times (n = 5, σ = 0.00110876), and s is the slope of the linear curve derived from the Stern-Volmer equation [13]. From the above equation, the limit of detection of the N-CDots for Fe3+ sensing was found to be 7.6 nM, which is comparable to or even lower than that of other reported carbon dots (Table 1 ).

To gain a deep insight into the quenching behavior of N-CDots, the time-resolved fluorescence was applied. The fluorescence decay behavior of N-CDots in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion was shown in Fig. 6 , and the exponential fit results are summarized in Table 2 . The lifetimes for pure N-CDots were analyzed using the double-exponential mode giving three decay lifetimes of 1.4435 ns, 4.7792 ns and 12.8264 ns (χ2 = 1.115). After the addition of 5 μM or 50 μM Fe3+ ion, the lifetimes changed to be 1.5895 ns, 5.1657 ns, 13.5316 ns (χ2 = 1.042) and 1.9427 ns, 5.8146 ns, 15.1842 ns (χ2 = 1.182), respectively. The changes in average lifetimes after the addition of different concentration of Fe3+ ion seems to indicate that the changes in fluorescence are due to dynamic quenching [35] (in Table 2). Because the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDots is significantly reduced with the addition of 50 μM Fe3+ ion, it illustrates that quenching of fluorescence upon Fe3+ binding arises from an electron transferring from the N-CDots fluorophore to the metal center [41]. The decrease of the fluorescence with increasing concentration of Fe3+ means that the addition of Fe3+ would change the fluorescence emission maximum or the lifetime of N-CDots. The fluorescence quenching mechanism schematic of N-CDots is shown in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDots for detecting Fe3+ tap water
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical feasibility of N-CDots, the concentration of Fe3+ in real sample of tap water is tested. Before the test, the samples were standing for 72 h at 25 °C and then filtered through a membrane filter (pore size 0.22 μm) to remove the impurities and gas from tap water, and the pH of the tap water sample was adjusted to 7.0 using the solution of HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). The results obtained from N-CDots were compared to the values from AAS (Atomic Absorption Spectrometer) in Table 3 . As it is can be seen, the data obtained by the two methods matched with each other. As shown in Table 4 , good recoveries ranging from 99.72 to 100.60% were obtained in the tap water samples by this N-CDots using standard addition method. From this result it can be concluded that the N-CDots are not only sensitive and selective but also reliable, which can be applied to simple and rapid detect of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of N-CDots
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is always one of the most important concerns of PL nanomaterial for optical cellular imaging. The toxicity of N-CDots to 3T3 and HepG2 cells were investigated by counting the viability of cells incubated with a series concentration of N-CDots for 24 h via the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 8 a, both kinds of cells show a cell viability to be over 90% after being incubated with N-CDots at concentrations of 50 μg mL−1, 100 μg mL−1 and 150 μg mL−1, which is over 70% even at a high concentration (1000 μg mL−1). N-CDots show a comparably even better biocompatibility than those reported CDots used as the fluorescent [5].

On the basis of the low toxicity and notable fluorescence performance, the N-CDots can be loaded into the CT26.WT cells and emits bright fluorescence in blue, green and red for multicolor imaging of cells. The multicolor fluorescence from N-CDots could be easily detected, with Fig. 8b suggesting, that the N-CDots reside mostly in the cell membrane and cytoplasm. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDots is relatively high compared with that of reported fluorescence samples (shown in Table 5 ), which is very favorable for the persistence of cell imaging. The paper proposes a nitrogen-rich carbon dots with colorful emission performing well in both Fe3+ detection and cellular imaging, and the results in the paper prove the possibility of multifunctionality of a probe made of N-CDots. Which make them potential candidates for imaging in biosensors and vivo study.

We have creatively synthesized one kind of nitrogen-rich CDots (N/C nearly to 39.9%) showing superior fluorescence properties and thus proving the multifunctional probing ability of CDots applied in environmental areas and potentially in vivo. As is expected, the N-CDots showed great selectivity, high sensitivity and were used as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 7.6 nM. In addition, the N-CDots showing really low toxicity but bright fluorescence imaging in blue to red were successfully applied for living cell imaging. These results demonstrated that N-CDots as one kind of precise probes can be applied in a variety of situations, such as environmental sensing and in vitro imaging.
Ying Liang, Yamin Liu, Siyuan Li, Bin Lu, Cailin Liu, Haijun Yang, Xianyan Ren*, Yiling Hou declares no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was supported by the Southwest University of Science and Technology of the Startup Foundation for Doctors, China [16ZX7139]; the longshan Talent Program, China [17LZX675]; and Postgraduate Innovation Fund Project by Southwest University of Science and Technology, China [18YCX069].

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of nanoparticles commonly used in fluorescent bioimaging
Luminescent films for chemo- and biosensing
Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion

Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink
Habitat evaluation of wild Amur tiger (Panthera tigris altaica) and conservation priority setting in north-eastern China
A novel polydentate ligand chromophore for simultaneously colorimetric detection of trace Ag+ and Fe3+
D-penicillamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ in iron supplement oral liquids
A novel method for the filterless preconcentration of iron
Chemical speciation of iron in seawater by cathodic stripping voltammetry with dihydroxynaphthalene
Determination of 13 synthetic food colorants in water-soluble foods by reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with diode-array detector
Colorimetric detection of Fe(III) ions using label-free gold nanoparticles and acidic thiourea mixture
Highly fluorescent silver oxide/C- dots nanocomposite as selective and sensitive probe for highly efficient detection of Fe(III) ions
Graphene quantum dots modified with adenine for efficient two-photon bioimaging and white light-activated antibacteria
Facile preparation of gadolinium(iii) chelates functionalized carbon quantum dot-based contrast agent for magnetic resonance/fluorescence multimodal imaging
Fluorescence imaging of cellular metabolites with RNA
Real-time imaging of tumor-cell shedding and trafficking in lymphatic channels
Super-resolution fluorescence imaging of organelles in live cells with photoswitchable membrane probes
Recent advances in super-resolution fluorescence imaging and its applications in biology
Molecular imaging: current status and emerging strategies
Adenovirus-directed expression of TIPE2 suppresses gastric cancer growth via induction of apoptosis and inhibition of AKT and ERK1/2 signaling
Effects of chemical bonds between nitrogen and its neighbor carbon atoms on fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots
Highly fluorescent graphene oxide-poly(vinyl alcohol) hybrid: an effective material for specific Au3+ ion sensor
The role of the lateral dimension of graphene oxide in the regulation of cellular responses
In vitro toxicity evaluation of graphene oxide on A549 cells
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from microcrystalline cellulose for the detection of Fe3+ ions in an acidic environment
Drug-derived bright and color-tunable N-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in living cells
Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate

Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
Preparation of carbon quantum dots from cigarette filters and its application for fluorescence detection of Sudan I
Excitation wavelength dependence of fluorescence intermittency in CdSe/ZnS core/shell quantum dots
Carbon dots prepared by solid state method via citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline for selective and sensing detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe(3+) sensing and cellular imaging
Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Fluorescence lifetime standards for time and frequency domain fluorescence spectroscopy
Rapid solid-phase microwave synthesis of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe(3+) detection and cellular bioimaging
Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid
Multimodal bioimaging based on gold nanorod and carbon dot nanohybrids as a novel tool for atherosclerosis detection
Anomalous enhancement of fluorescence of carbon dots through lanthanum doping and potential application in intracellular imaging of ferric ion
The influence of dead time related distortions on live cell fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM) experiments
